title: Pampushky
subtitle: Ukrainian garlic rolls
excerpt: Soft, pillowy yeasted rolls brushed with garlic-and-dill oil straight from the oven. The traditional Ukrainian bread served alongside borscht — more flavour than a dinner roll, simpler to make than a flatbread.
sourceNotes: Pampushky are the traditional bread accompaniment to Ukrainian borscht. They are small, soft, yeasted rolls, brushed generously with sunflower oil infused with garlic and dill while still hot from the oven, so the garlic flavour cooks slightly into the crust. They are eaten by tearing them open and dipping into the bowl of borscht. Outside the borscht pairing they are served as a bread course at Ukrainian meals and at celebrations.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Reheat at 160°C for 8 minutes. Add the garlic oil after reheating rather than before freezing.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Make in large batches and freeze.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be shaped into rolls, placed on the tray, and refrigerated overnight. Bring to room temperature (1 hour) before baking.

content.text: Pampushky dough is similar to a soft dinner roll dough but made with water and oil rather than milk and butter, keeping it vegan. The rolls are baked until light golden (not as dark as you might take a bread roll), then immediately brushed with a generous amount of raw garlic oil. The heat of the rolls cooks the raw garlic slightly as it soaks in, softening its sharpness while keeping the flavour strong.
The garlic oil is applied hot, not after the rolls have cooled. If you wait, it sits on the surface; applied while the rolls are still steaming, it is absorbed into the crust.

Mix the {{yeast-dried}} and {{granulated-sugar}} into 220 ml of warm water and leave for 5 minutes. Add the {{plain-flour}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and 3 tablespoons of {{sunflower-oil}}. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic. Cover and leave to rise in a warm place for 60 minutes until doubled.

Divide the dough into 12 equal pieces. Roll each into a smooth ball and place close together (but not touching) on a lined baking tray. Cover loosely and leave to prove for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 200°C. Bake for 18 to 20 minutes until lightly golden. Mix the {{garlic}}, 4 tablespoons of {{sunflower-oil}}, {{dill}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} to make the garlic oil. Brush the rolls all over immediately when they come out of the oven. Leave for 5 minutes before serving.

items.symptom: Rolls dense and not soft inside
items.cause: Under-risen, or water too hot and killed the yeast
items.fix: Give the dough the full 60 minutes to rise — it should be clearly doubled. Water temperature should be warm to the touch (37°C), not hot

items.symptom: Garlic oil sitting on the surface rather than absorbing
items.cause: Rolls not brushed immediately from the oven
items.fix: Brush within 30 seconds of removing from the oven while the rolls are still steaming. The moisture draws the oil in

items.cause: Garlic not crushed finely enough, or too much garlic
items.fix: Use a garlic press or crush to a smooth paste before mixing with the oil. The heat from the rolls cooks the garlic slightly, but a coarse chop will not soften in the same way

content.text: Pampushky are borscht bread. Across Ukraine, a bowl of borscht arrives at the table alongside a plate of pampushky — they are not listed on the menu separately because they are not served separately. You tear them apart and dip them into the borscht, or eat them alongside as a vehicle for the broth. At celebrations and at Christmas Eve (Svyata Vechera), pampushky appear as part of the bread course. They are also made on ordinary days when soup is on the table and a soft, garlicky roll is wanted.
